text:
Admission Date:  1998-03-24              Discharge Date:   1992-01-09

Date of Birth:  1993-11-09             Sex:   M

Service: MEDICINE

Allergies:
Patient recorded as having No Known Allergies to Drugs

Attending:James
Chief Complaint:
Seizure, EtOH withdrawal

Major Surgical or Invasive Procedure:
None


History of Present Illness:
History of Present Illness: Mr. Jackson is a 48 year old man with
h/o EtOH abuse, depression with psychotic features, HTN, and
hypercholesterolemia who was referred to the ED by his PCP for
abnormal LFTs. Pt was seen by his PCP Martin 1993-01-07 for routine BP
follow-up. At that time, LFTs and lipid panel were checked as
simvastatin dose increased from 20mg to 40mg approximately 2
weeks ago. These returned with ALT 167, AST 676, and TG 3622.
Repeat labs on 1983-08-05 revealed similar results with ALT 165, AST
465, and TG 4763. He was asked to present to the ED here for
further evaluation.

In the ED, initial VS were: T 97.6, P 106, BP 157/113, R 17, O2
sat 100% on RA. Labs notable for ALT 241, AST 809, and Alk phos
223 but normal Tbili. Abdominal ultrasound showed diffusely
echogenic liver without focal mass lesion; unable to exclude
cirrhosis or fibrosis. Pt unexpectedly had a 5-minute episode of
tonic-clonic seizure which was terminated with lorazepam 2mg IV.
Initally placed on NRB due to concern for apneic breathing but
resolved. CT head showed no acute intracranial findings. In
retrospect, pt did have mild tremor on exam although was not
agitated. He admitted to wkend drinking with last drink
reportedly 10 beers on 1987-02-25. He was started on CIWA scale and
received diazepam 10mg IV x 2 (at 2:40pm and 3:45pm). He also
received IV thiamine, folate, and MVI via banana bag. On
transfer, VS: T 99.1, P 75, BP 157/86, R 15, O2 sat 100% on 2L
NC.

On the floor, pt denies feeling anxious. No known h/o seizures
or head trauma. He again reports drinking only 10 beers on
Fridays and Saturdays although he does admit to depression with
psychotic features for which he "occasionally" self-medicates
with 3-4 beers. However, per family, he has a long history of
EtOH abuse and drinks vodka and/or beer daily - "whatever he can
get." He has been forced to go to detox multiple times by law
(last 2 years ago) but has always relapsed. His last drink was
Monday AM as he knew he would need to come to the ED today. No
h/o EtOH withdrawal, or DTs in the past.


Past Medical History:
- Hypertension
- Hypercholesterolemia
- Depression with psychotic features: Reports auditory
hallucinations at times telling him to hurt himself but denies
h/o SI/HI.
- EtOH abuse
- Tobacco abuse


Social History:
Lives with parents, brother, and nephews. John Madden in construction.
EtOH use as above. Smokes 10-18 ppd x 15 years. Remote h/o
marijuana; denies h/o IVDU.


Family History:
Brother with depression

Physical Exam:
Vitals: T 98.5, BP 144/92, P 73, RR 14, O2sat 96%RA
General: Alert, oriented x 3, no acute distress
HEENT: Sclera anicteric, MMM, oropharynx clear
Neck: Supple, JVP not elevated, no LAD
Lungs: Clear to auscultation bilaterally, no wheezes, rales,
rhonchi
CV: Regular rate and rhythm, normal S1 + S2, no murmurs, rubs,
gallops
Abdomen: Soft, non-tender, non-distended, bowel sounds present,
no rebound tenderness or guarding, no organomegaly
GU: No foley
Ext: Warm, well perfused, 2+ pulses, +clubbing, no cyanosis or
edema
Skin: No jaundice
Neuro: Mild tremor, otherwise nonfocal.
Psych: Denies anxiety, SI/HI.


Pertinent Results:
Chemistries:

POTASSIUM-4.4 CHLORIDE-95* TOTAL CO2-26 ANION GAP-16

EOS-0.1 BASOS-0.6

cocaine-NEG amphetmn-NEG mthdone-NEG

GLUCOSE-NEG KETONE-NEG BILIRUBIN-NEG UROBILNGN-NEG PH-6.5
LEUK-NEG

Imaging:
RUQ Ultrasound: Diffusely echogenic liver without focal mass
lesion. More
advanced forms of liver disease such as cirrhosis or fibrosis
not excluded.

CT Head: 1.No acute intracranial hemorrhage or mass effect. If
there is concern for intracranial cause to explain the patient's
symptoms, consider MR head.  2. Small foci of air in the sella
and adjacent cavernous sinus region- unclear significance given
the lack of trauma. This may relate to subtle dehiscence of the
sphenoid sinus wall. F/u if clinically indictaed.

AP AND LATERAL CHEST: The positioning is slightly lordotic.
Heart size is
normal. The mediastinal and hilar contours normal. The lungs are
clear.
There is no pneumothorax or pleural effusion.




Brief Hospital Course:
Assessment and Plan: 48 year old man with history of ethanol
abuse, depression with psychotic features, admitted with
seizure, likely secondary to ethanol withdrawal.
.
1. Seizure/EtOH withdrawal:  The patient who presented to the
emergency department for evaluation of high liver enzymes had an
unexpected tonic clonic seizure lasting approximately five
minutes and was terminated after administration of 2 mg of
Ativan. The patient was briefly placed on a non-rebreather due
to concern for respiratory compromise, but he did not need
further oxygen requirements. The seizure was felt to be due to
alcohol withdrawal as it occurred less than 36 hours after his
last drink.  Other obvious acute causes were ruled out. The
patient stayed one night in the MICU with no further incident. A
CIWA protocol was in place and he required approximately 40 mg
of diazepam for the entirety of his hospital stay. His symptoms
during his withdrawal consisted of tremor and nausea. Upon
discharge, the patient had no signs of active withdrawal. He was
not interested in going to a detoxification facility.

.
2. Transaminitis: On admission patient had elevated AST (809)
and ALT (241).  A right upper quadrant ultrasound was notable
for a diffusely echogenic liver without focal mass. He was also
noted to have a recent increase in his dosage of simvastatin
from 20 mg to 40 mg daily. Upon stopping his statin and
detoxing, his enzymes decreased. Liver enzymes on discharge were
AST:226 and ALT:125. The elevated levels are likely largely due
to excessive alcoholic intake, but the statin effect can not be
ruled out. His Hepatitis surface Ab was positive, with core and
Ag negative, suggesting immunity and no active infection.
Hepatitis C was negative. Patient was strongly advised to
abstain from alcohol. Patient should follow-up with his primary
care doctor for further management.
.
3. Hypertriglyceridemia/Dyspilipidemia: Patient has been stable
and has had no acute cardiac or pancreatic dysfunction. Likely
secondary to Alcohol abuse. Patient was started on fish oil as
all other therapies are liver toxic. He should follow-up with
his primary doctor for further management.
.
4. Thrombocytopenia: Patient remained stable with no signs of
active bleeding. His platelet count ranged from 140-150. No
intervention was needed at these levels. This is likely
secondary to alcohol abuse, although other causes cannot be
ruled out. He should be followed up in the outpatient.
.
5. Major Depression with Psychotic Features: Stable. Patient
denied suicidal and homicidal ideations and hallucinations
through the course of his admission. He was continued on
Quetiapine and Fluoxetine.
.
6. Tobacco abuse: The patient was started on a nicotine patch
and was advised to quit smoking.
.
7. Hypertension: Remained stable with no signs of organ damage.
He was continued on lisinopril and atenolol, his home doses. He
is to follow-up for further management with his PCP.


Medications on Admission:
1. Lisinopril 20 mg Tablet
2. Atenolol 50 mg Tablet
3. Simvistatin 40 mg daily
4. Quetiapine 100 mg Tablet
5. Fluoxetine 20 mg Capsule
6. Guanfacine 0.5mg qhs (unclear if taking)
7. Tylenol PRN


Discharge Medications:
1. Lisinopril 20 mg Tablet Sig: One (1) Tablet PO DAILY (Daily).

2. Atenolol 50 mg Tablet Sig: One (1) Tablet PO DAILY (Daily).
3. Multivitamin     Tablet Sig: One (1) Tablet PO DAILY (Daily).

4. Thiamine HCl 100 mg Tablet Sig: One (1) Tablet PO DAILY
(Daily).
5. Folic Acid 1 mg Tablet Sig: One (1) Tablet PO DAILY (Daily).

6. Quetiapine 100 mg Tablet Sig: One (1) Tablet PO HS (at
bedtime).
7. Fluoxetine 20 mg Capsule Sig: One (1) Capsule PO DAILY
(Daily).
8. Omega-3 Fatty Acids     Capsule Sig: 10-18 Capsules PO BID (2
times a day).
Disp:*120 Capsule(s)* Refills:*2*


Discharge Disposition:
Home

Discharge Diagnosis:
Primary:
1. Alcohol Abuse
2. Hypertriglyceridemia
Secondary:
1. Dyslipidemia
2. Transaminitis
3. Thrombocytopenia


Discharge Condition:
Stable, no signs of active withdrawal.


Discharge Instructions:
You were admitted because you had a seizure in the emergency
room. We performed a CT scan of your head which did not show
anything acute to cause your seizure. Based on your history and
your blood tests, the seizure was likely due to alcohol
withdrawal. You were admitted to the intensive care unit for
close monitoring. We gave you medicines to prevent you from
having a seizure again. All of your signs of alcohol withdrawal
have subsided.

 You also have very high cholesterol, triglycerides, and liver
enzymes in your blood. This is likely due to your excessive
alcohol intake. You must stop drinking alcohol if you want to
improve these numbers. If you do not, you run a very high risk
of heart disease, pancreatic disease, liver disease, and even
death.

 You are strongly advised to stop drinking.

Your new medications:
Fish Oil 1000 mg tablets; take 10-18 pills by mouth twice a day
(this will help your triglyceride levels)

You should go to the emergency room or contact your primary care
doctor if you experience any significant abdominal pain, chest
pain, shortness of breath, seizures, hallucinations, or any
other symptom that is concerning to you.

Followup Instructions:
You are scheduled to see
Dr. Kim Diane Johnson
Date and time: 2000-07-18 2:00pm
Location: Anna Jaques Hospital
Phone number: 704-642-5803
